questions.prompt: What is the essence of the 'racing line' concept in drone racing?
options: Always following the shortest straight line between gates
Finishing the track in the shortest time with the smoothest (most flowing) line, preserving momentum
Reaching the highest instantaneous speed possible
Skipping as many gates as possible to take a shortcut
questions.explanation: The goal isn't the highest instantaneous speed or the shortest straight line, but finishing the whole track fastest with the smoothest line that preserves momentum.

questions.prompt: Why is a cornered line (hard braking at each gate) usually slower than a smooth line?
options: Because the cornered line is longer
Because every hard brake destroys accumulated momentum, and regaining it takes time
Because the drone burns more fuel on a cornered line
Because the cornered line is forbidden
questions.explanation: Hard brake + hard turn + re-accelerate burns momentum. A smooth line carries most of the speed through, so the total time is shorter.

questions.prompt: What is the ideal situation when exiting a race gate?
options: The drone's nose already being aligned with / pointing at the next gate
Coming to a complete stop right after the gate
Turning back to check the gate after each one
The exit angle doesn't matter at all
questions.explanation: Your exit angle from one gate determines your entry line into the next. Ideally you're already aligned with the next target as you exit; a good pilot reads ahead.

questions.prompt: How should throttle be managed in racing?
options: Held at full throttle (pinned) constantly
Used only as on or off (on/off)
Modulated like a tap: opened on straights and eased off smoothly before turns and gates
Held at a single constant value throughout the entire track
questions.explanation: Constant full throttle makes control harder and throws the drone off line. Throttle is modulated like a tap: opened on straights, eased off before turns, and opened again on exit.

questions.prompt: Why is consistency more valuable than raw speed in racing?
options: Because flying consistently uses less battery
Because the rules ban speed
Because a blazing lap followed by a crash/miss means recovery time or a DNF; a clean, repeatable line gives a shorter total time
Because consistency automatically doubles speed
questions.explanation: A pilot who pushes past the limit and crashes every lap finishes later than one who flies a bit slower but clean. Clean, consistent lines first; speed is added on top of that gradually.
